metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `build_payload` (completion_sidecar.py) to the Scope section and specify: (a) a new schema sentinel for PASS records (e.g. `completion_verifier_pass_v1`), (b) that `build_payload` must include `criteria[]` in the PASS payload, and (c) that `latest_pass_record` must filter on the new PASS schema sentinel — not the existing `completion_verifier_fail_v1` string.
evidence: Plan Scope: 'completion_sidecar.py — emit on PASS as well as FAIL; bump schema; add a latest_pass_record reader' — build_payload is not mentioned
completion_sidecar.py:145: 'def build_payload(verdict: dict[str, Any], *, material: str | None = None) -> dict[str, Any]: The slim, queryable sidecar payload for a completion FAIL verdict.'
completion_sidecar.py:29: SCHEMA = 'completion_verifier_fail_v1'  # name embeds 'fail'
completion_sidecar.py:47: payload = build_payload(verdict, material=material)  # emit() calls build_payload directly
ABSENCE: build_payload does not appear in the plan's Scope section or Acceptance Criteria
findings.finding: The plan's Scope section lists three files to modify (completion_verdict.schema.json, completion.py, completion_sidecar.py) and asserts 'emit on PASS as well as FAIL', but omits build_payload — the function emit() directly calls — from the explicit change surface. build_payload's docstring explicitly scopes it to FAIL verdicts ('the slim, queryable sidecar payload for a completion FAIL verdict'), the SCHEMA constant is 'completion_verifier_fail_v1', and the function currently calls reconcile_verdict which strips non-findings fields. Extending emit() to PASS without correspondingly specifying updates to build_payload leaves a critical intermediary unaddressed; a developer following the Scope section as written would produce a PASS-path emit that calls a FAIL-only payload builder, yielding a payload with the wrong schema sentinel and no criteria[] field.
scenarios: Developer extends emit() to fire on PASS but does not update build_payload — the emitted payload carries schema='completion_verifier_fail_v1' and omits criteria[], defeating the whole purpose of the PASS-path audit record.
latest_pass_record reader filters on the old 'completion_verifier_fail_v1' schema sentinel, returning None for all PASS records if build_payload is not updated to emit a new schema string.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope bullet for completion_sidecar.py, add: 'Update build_payload to accept/emit a per-verdict schema sentinel (completion_verifier_pass_v1 on PASS, completion_verifier_fail_v1 on FAIL) and include criteria[] in the PASS payload; update latest_pass_record to filter on completion_verifier_pass_v1.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py` to the Scope edit list and specify where on the PASS path `completion_sidecar.emit()` is called (e.g., after the `_verdict_manifest` call, before signing).
evidence: completion_sidecar.emit is called only at transition_close.py:238, inside `if str(result.get('verdict', '')).upper() != 'PASS':`
The PASS path (lines 247-256) calls only `_verdict_manifest` — no sidecar emit
Plan Scope lists only completion_verdict.schema.json, completion.py, completion_sidecar.py
AC-2 says 'A completion PASS emits a completion sidecar event' — the trigger site is transition_close.py
findings.finding: transition_close.py is an unenumerated consumer that must be edited to emit the PASS sidecar. The PASS path currently just calls `_verdict_manifest` and returns; `completion_sidecar.emit()` is only called on the FAIL branch (line 238). Emitting on PASS requires a new call-site on the PASS path in this file, which the Scope does not name.
scenarios: Implementation proceeds touching only the three named files; the PASS sidecar is never actually emitted because no call-site exists on the PASS path.
findings.suggested_fix: In Scope, add: `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py` — on the PASS path (after verdict confirmed PASS, before `_verdict_manifest`), call `completion_sidecar.emit(result, material=material, repo_root=repo_root)` with best-effort semantics mirroring the FAIL path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/contracts.py` (the `CompletionVerdict` pydantic model) to Scope as an edit target, specifying an optional `criteria: list[CriterionResult] = []` field to match the new schema array.
evidence: contracts.py:75 defines `class CompletionVerdict(BaseModel)` with only `verdict`, `findings`, `summary` fields
runner.py:125 calls `_findings.validate_structured(dict(self._structured), req.output_schema)` using this model
Plan Scope does not name contracts.py
Plan says 'update the verifier prompt to emit it [criteria[]]' but the structured model gates what the LLM can actually return
findings.finding: src/rebar/llm/contracts.py (CompletionVerdict pydantic model) is an unenumerated edit target. The plan requires the verifier prompt to emit a criteria[] array, but the structured-output model at contracts.py:75 has no `criteria` field. The runner validates the LLM's response against this model before reconcile_verdict ever sees it — a model field missing here means the agent's criteria[] is silently stripped before it reaches any downstream consumer.
scenarios: Developer updates the prompt and schema but not contracts.py; the pydantic model strips criteria[] on agent output validation, so reconcile_verdict never sees it and the PASS sidecar always has an empty criteria[].

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/completion-verification.yaml`, `src/rebar/schemas/completion_reconcile_input.schema.json`, and `src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_ops.py` (`completion_reconcile`) to Scope as edit targets for threading `criteria[]` from agent output to reconciled verdict.
evidence: completion-verification.yaml lines 58-64 wire only: ticket_id, raw_verdict, raw_findings, runner, model, trace_id, certifiable
completion_reconcile_input.schema.json has `additionalProperties: false` and no criteria field
gate_ops.py:125-133 builds result dict from ctx.inputs with no criteria field
Plan Scope does not name any of these three files
findings.finding: The completion-verification.yaml workflow and gate_ops.py (completion_reconcile step) are unenumerated consumers that must be updated to thread criteria[] from the agent's output through to the reconciled verdict. The YAML wires only `raw_verdict`, `raw_findings`, `runner`, `model`, `trace_id` from the verify step; `completion_reconcile_input.schema.json` has `additionalProperties: false` with no `raw_criteria` field.
scenarios: Developer adds criteria[] to completion_verdict schema and contracts.py but not to the YAML wiring or reconcile_input schema; the workflow path produces a verdict with no criteria[] even on PASS (additionalProperties: false would block raw_criteria being passed through).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in Scope whether the schema bump introduces a new constant (e.g., `SCHEMA_PASS = 'completion_verifier_pass_v1'`) rather than renaming the existing one, and update `latest_fail_verdict`'s schema guard accordingly — or explain the migration strategy for the reader.
evidence: completion_sidecar.py:29: `SCHEMA = 'completion_verifier_fail_v1'`
completion_sidecar.py:133: `if isinstance(payload, dict) and payload.get('schema') == SCHEMA:`
Plan says 'bump schema' without specifying the new value or reader migration
test_completion_gate.py:538: `assert rec['schema'] == 'completion_verifier_fail_v1'` — a pinned test would fail on any rename
findings.finding: The plan says 'bump schema' in completion_sidecar.py but the existing `latest_fail_verdict` reader guards on `payload.get('schema') == SCHEMA` (the module constant). A schema bump (e.g., to 'completion_verifier_v2') would cause `latest_fail_verdict` to reject all pre-bump FAIL records AND all post-bump records unless the guard is updated. The plan neither specifies the new schema string, nor how `latest_fail_verdict` should handle both old and new schema versions.
scenarios: Schema bump without reader update causes latest_fail_verdict to return None for all records, breaking existing FAIL observability.
A strict equality guard makes the reader unable to distinguish between 'no records' and 'records exist but with a different schema version'.
findings.suggested_fix: Introduce a separate `SCHEMA_PASS = 'completion_verifier_pass_v1'` constant for PASS records rather than bumping the shared SCHEMA, so FAIL records keep their existing schema sentinel and latest_fail_verdict requires no change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py` (and any other call sites, e.g. `gate_dispatch.py`) to the Scope, specifying that the PASS branch must call `completion_sidecar.emit()` after a successful verification, analogous to how the FAIL branch already does.
evidence: Scope says: 'completion_sidecar.py — emit on PASS as well as FAIL' but does not list transition_close.py

transition_close.py lines 234-241: emit() is called inside the FAIL branch (`if str(result.get('verdict', '')).upper() != 'PASS':`); the PASS path (lines 242+) proceeds directly to signing with no emit
The PASS path cannot gain a sidecar emit solely through changes in completion_sidecar.py — a new call site in transition_close.py (or gate_dispatch.py) is required
findings.finding: The PASS sidecar emit call site (`transition_close.py`) is absent from the plan's Scope, making the 'emit on PASS' mechanism incomplete: the FAIL emit lives in `transition_close.py` inside the `if verdict != 'PASS'` branch; the PASS branch (lines 242+) has no emit call and is not listed as a file to change.
scenarios: Implementer only modifies completion_sidecar.py as listed in Scope; no PASS emit is ever wired up; AC2 ('A completion PASS emits a completion sidecar event') is unachievable without touching transition_close.py
findings.suggested_fix: In `transition_close.py`, after the FAIL branch's `raise CommandError`, add a best-effort `completion_sidecar.emit(result, material=material_fingerprint, repo_root=repo_root)` call in the PASS continuation (before or after signing). List `transition_close.py` in the plan's Scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Scope whether PASS and FAIL sidecar records use the same or separate schema tags (e.g., keep `'completion_verifier_fail_v1'` for FAILs and introduce `'completion_verifier_pass_v1'` for PASSes), and describe how `latest_fail_verdict`'s schema guard and the new `latest_pass_record` reader each filter by tag.
evidence: `SCHEMA = 'completion_verifier_fail_v1'` in completion_sidecar.py line 29
`latest_fail_verdict` guard: `payload.get('schema') == SCHEMA` (line 133) — a strict equality check keyed on the module constant
Plan says 'bump schema' without specifying new string(s), whether FAIL and PASS records share or diverge, or how reader guards are updated
findings.finding: The plan says 'bump schema' in `completion_sidecar.py` but does not specify the new schema string(s) or how the existing `latest_fail_verdict` schema-equality guard (`payload.get('schema') == SCHEMA`) behaves when the constant changes — creating an ambiguous edge case where either (a) the guard silently drops old FAIL records (if `SCHEMA` is renamed) or (b) PASS records carry a semantically misleading `'completion_verifier_fail_v1'` tag.
scenarios: Implementer renames SCHEMA to 'completion_verifier_v2'; existing FAIL sidecar records (written with 'completion_verifier_fail_v1') are silently skipped by latest_fail_verdict, breaking offline FAIL inspection
Implementer uses the same SCHEMA for both PASS and FAIL records; PASS records carry a 'fail' label, confusing future readers
findings.suggested_fix: Introduce a second schema constant (e.g., `PASS_SCHEMA = 'completion_verifier_pass_v1'`) for PASS records, keep `SCHEMA = 'completion_verifier_fail_v1'` unchanged for FAIL records, and have `latest_pass_record` filter on `PASS_SCHEMA`. This preserves the existing FAIL reader guard with zero churn.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the schema version strategy for `completion_sidecar.py`: decide whether FAIL events keep `"completion_verifier_fail_v1"` (only PASS events get a new schema tag), or both migrate to a unified new schema string with `latest_fail_verdict` updated to walk-back over both versions — and document it in the Scope section so the implementation cannot silently break the existing FAIL reader.
evidence: Plan text: 'completion_sidecar.py — emit on PASS as well as FAIL; bump schema; add a latest_pass_record reader'
completion_sidecar.py line 29: `SCHEMA = "completion_verifier_fail_v1"`
completion_sidecar.py line 133: `payload.get("schema") == SCHEMA` — the reader only accepts this one literal string
test_completion_gate.py line 538: `assert rec["schema"] == "completion_verifier_fail_v1"` — existing test pins the schema literal
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'bump schema' in completion_sidecar.py but leaves the version strategy unresolved. The existing `latest_fail_verdict` reader schema-guards on the hard-coded literal `"completion_verifier_fail_v1"` (completion_sidecar.py line 133). If all newly-emitted COMPLETION_VERDICT events (PASS and FAIL alike) carry a bumped schema string, `latest_fail_verdict` will silently return None for every post-bump FAIL record, breaking the existing caller in `test_fail_close_persists_durable_verdict` and leaving the FAIL observability path dark. The plan names a new `latest_pass_record` reader without specifying whether the old reader is updated, versioned-branched, or whether FAIL events keep the old schema string.
findings.location: Scope section — `src/rebar/llm/completion_sidecar.py` bullet ('bump schema; add a `latest_pass_record` reader'); completion_sidecar.py line 133 (`payload.get("schema") == SCHEMA`)
scenarios: After the schema bump, a FAIL close emits a COMPLETION_VERDICT with the new schema string. `latest_fail_verdict` returns None (schema guard rejects it). The existing test `test_fail_close_persists_durable_verdict` silently breaks.
Operators querying past FAIL records via `latest_fail_verdict` after the upgrade see None for any record emitted post-bump.
findings.suggested_fix: Keep `"completion_verifier_fail_v1"` for FAIL events (schema unchanged, reader unchanged, existing test unchanged). Emit PASS events with a new `"completion_verifier_pass_v1"` schema string, read by a new `latest_pass_record` that guards on that literal. This is the minimum-diff path that adds the PASS record without touching any existing FAIL consumer.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the new PASS sidecar schema version string (e.g. `"completion_verifier_pass_v1"`) and specify in the plan how `latest_pass_record` discriminates PASS from FAIL events (e.g. by schema string equality on the new literal).
evidence: completion_sidecar.py line 29: `SCHEMA = "completion_verifier_fail_v1"`
completion_sidecar.py lines 132-134: `latest_fail_verdict` guards `payload.get('schema') == SCHEMA` (exact string match)
Plan Scope: 'bump schema; add a `latest_pass_record` reader' — no new schema name or version defined
ABSENCE: no specification of how PASS and FAIL sidecar payloads sharing EVENT_TYPE='COMPLETION_VERDICT' are distinguished by either reader
findings.finding: The plan says 'bump schema' in completion_sidecar.py but leaves the new schema version string undefined and provides no discrimination protocol for `latest_pass_record`. The existing PASS sidecar reader (`latest_fail_verdict`) schema-guards on the hard-coded literal `"completion_verifier_fail_v1"`. If the PASS sidecar uses the same schema string, `latest_fail_verdict` will return PASS records to callers expecting FAILs; if a new string is used, its value is unspecified. `latest_pass_record` must also distinguish PASS from FAIL events sharing the same `EVENT_TYPE`, but no protocol is given.
findings.location: Scope — `src/rebar/llm/completion_sidecar.py` / plan phrase 'bump schema; add a `latest_pass_record` reader'
scenarios: If PASS sidecar reuses the same SCHEMA string, latest_fail_verdict returns PASS records as 'fail verdicts'
If a new SCHEMA string is introduced but unspecified, two implementors choose different strings and the reader breaks
findings.suggested_fix: Introduce a second schema constant `PASS_SCHEMA = "completion_verifier_pass_v1"` alongside the existing `SCHEMA = "completion_verifier_fail_v1"`, and have `latest_pass_record` guard on `payload.get('schema') == PASS_SCHEMA`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the plan a concrete specification of the verifier prompt addition (the output-section wording instructing the LLM to emit `criteria[]` with `criterion`, `met`, `citation`, `kind` per entry) and the corresponding `CompletionVerdict` Pydantic model field to be added in `contracts.py`.
evidence: completion_verifier.md lines 162-181: the 'Verdict and findings' section instructs the LLM to emit `verdict`, `findings`, and `summary` only — no mention of `criteria[]`
contracts.py lines 75-96: `CompletionVerdict` Pydantic model has fields `verdict`, `findings`, `summary` only — no `criteria` field

findings.finding: The verifier prompt (completion_verifier.md) is not updated in the plan to instruct the LLM to emit the new `criteria[]` array. The plan says 'update the verifier prompt to emit it' but provides no specification of what the prompt should say — specifically, what fields to include per entry, how `met` should be expressed, and how `citation` in a criteria entry differs from `citations` in a finding. The `CompletionVerdict` Pydantic model in contracts.py also lacks a `criteria` field; without updating both the prompt and the model, the LLM has no instruction to emit the field and the structured-output contract rejects or ignores it.
findings.location: Scope — `src/rebar/llm/completion.py` / plan phrase 'update the verifier prompt to emit it' / `src/rebar/llm/contracts.py` `CompletionVerdict`
scenarios: An executor adds `criteria` to the Pydantic model but the prompt never mentions it, so the LLM consistently omits it and the PASS sidecar always has an empty criteria[]
The LLM emits a differently shaped structure because the prompt uses different field names than the schema

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan (co-located with the reconcile_verdict scope bullet) the exact mutation rule: e.g. 'on PASS→FAIL flip, set all criteria[].met to False' or 'pass criteria[] through unchanged regardless of verdict flip'.
evidence: Plan Scope: 'thread `criteria[]` through `reconcile_verdict` (a populated `criteria[]` must NOT trip the PASS-with-findings→FAIL flip)'
completion.py lines 216-229: reconcile_verdict reads only `result.get('findings') or []`; no handling of a `criteria` key exists
ABSENCE: the plan specifies no concrete action (preserve, validate, clear on FAIL, etc.) for how reconcile_verdict treats criteria[]
findings.finding: The protocol for how `reconcile_verdict` handles the new `criteria[]` field is not co-located with the decision rule that must change. The plan states 'a populated `criteria[]` must NOT trip the PASS-with-findings→FAIL flip' but does not specify whether `reconcile_verdict` should pass `criteria[]` through untouched, validate it, strip it, or do nothing. The current code reads only `result.get('findings')` so the field would be silently ignored — but the plan also says to 'thread `criteria[]` through `reconcile_verdict`', leaving the threading protocol ambiguous.
scenarios: An executor adds a pass-through line but another part of the pipeline strips the field before sidecar emit
reconcile_verdict on a PASS→FAIL flip leaves criteria[] populated even though some entries may have met=True, creating an inconsistent record

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an Acceptance Criterion for `latest_pass_record`: state its observable contract (e.g., returns the most-recently-persisted PASS sidecar record for a given ticket) and provide a unit-test oracle (feed it a fixture with one or more PASS records and assert the returned value).
evidence: Scope says: 'add a `latest_pass_record` reader'
No AC mentions `latest_pass_record`, its interface, or any assertion that it returns the correct record.

Oracle: a unit test writing two fixture records and asserting the later one is returned; a second test asserting `None` on an empty store.'

evidence: Plan Scope: 'add a `latest_pass_record` reader' listed as a deliverable
AC1 oracle: asserts `reconcile_verdict` behavior — does not read the sidecar
AC2 oracle: 'asserting on the emitted event JSON' — does not call the reader

`latest_fail_verdict` grep: only 2 test call-sites (tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_completion_gate.py:536, :575), zero production call-sites — the proposed `latest_pass_record` would start with none
findings.finding: `latest_pass_record` is listed as a Scope deliverable in `completion_sidecar.py` but no current plan consumer (production call-site or non-test AC) is identified that reads it — the three ACs only assert on the emitted event JSON and the persisted sidecar payload, not on a reader return value. `latest_fail_verdict` (its direct parallel) has zero production call-sites and only 2 test call-sites; `latest_pass_record` starts with none. Adding a reader with no current consumer is a YAGNI signal under the Rule-of-Three / YAGNI criterion.
scenarios: The reader is added as dead code: it ships, passes lint/typecheck, but no caller ever invokes it until a future ticket needs it — adding speculative API surface now.
If a downstream consumer IS planned (e.g. a CLI command or remediation flow that reads PASS records), naming it here without specifying that consumer makes the AC unverifiable.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) name the current consumer in the Scope/AC (e.g. 'the CLI `verify-completion` output path reads it to display per-criterion audit'), or (b) drop `latest_pass_record` from this ticket's scope and add it in the ticket that first needs to call it — the emitted sidecar event is already queryable via raw JSON without a reader.

scenarios: A future engineer modifying completion_sidecar.py doesn't know the audit contract exists or why criteria[] is a separate channel from findings
The PASS sidecar pattern is re-invented or broken in a follow-on change because the design rationale lives only in this plan ticket, not in a persistent doc
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-paragraph entry to CLAUDE.md (or a new `docs/adr/completion-pass-audit.md`) describing: (1) why criteria[] is separate from findings, (2) what the PASS sidecar guarantees, and (3) rollback semantics. Reference it from the Scope section of this plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify error handling for the new PASS sidecar write: confirm write failures degrade gracefully (verdict propagates, failure is logged/metered), and clarify idempotency semantics (overwrite vs. append) for re-runs on the same ticket.
evidence: Plan says 'emit on PASS as well as FAIL; bump schema; add a latest_pass_record reader' with no mention of failure modes
No statement that a sidecar write failure on PASS degrades gracefully (verdict still propagates) vs. raises
No mention of logging or metrics for the new emission path
Existing FAIL sidecar error handling is not referenced or confirmed to apply to the PASS path
findings.finding: The plan adds a new sidecar write on the PASS path but specifies no error-handling behavior for write failures on that path, nor confirms it inherits the existing FAIL sidecar's error handling.
findings.location: src/rebar/llm/completion_sidecar.py — 'emit on PASS as well as FAIL'
scenarios: Sidecar write fails (disk full, permissions) on a PASS — does the verdict propagate to the caller or does an unhandled exception corrupt the flow?
Repeated runs on the same ticket: does the PASS sidecar overwrite, append, or error on a pre-existing record?
findings.suggested_fix: In the completion_sidecar.py scope bullet, add: 'PASS sidecar writes are best-effort — a write failure logs a warning and emits a metric but does not raise; subsequent runs overwrite the existing record (last-writer-wins, keyed on ticket+runner+material fingerprint).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a RETIRE/retention policy for PASS sidecar records (e.g., overwrite-in-place per ticket, keep N most recent, or explicit TTL) and specify how `latest_pass_record` resolves when multiple records exist.
evidence: 'emit on PASS as well as FAIL; bump schema; add a latest_pass_record reader' — 'latest' implies multiple records can exist per ticket, but no pruning or retention is specified
ABSENCE: no mention of record retention, maximum accumulation, or cleanup for PASS sidecars
'No backfill' addresses historical records only, not forward accumulation
findings.finding: The PASS sidecar emits a new record for every passing ticket run, but no RETIRE/cleanup/retention policy is specified. Over time (and across re-runs/retries of the same ticket), PASS sidecar records will accumulate with no defined expiry or pruning path.
scenarios: A ticket that is run through the verifier many times (CI retries, re-reviews) accumulates unbounded PASS sidecar records
Storage grows silently with no operator visibility or control
findings.suggested_fix: Specify that `completion_sidecar.py` writes the PASS sidecar to a fixed, deterministic path per ticket (same path each run, overwriting), making it idempotent and eliminating accumulation. Document this in the Scope section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that PASS sidecar writes are atomic (e.g., write to a temp file then rename, or document that single-writer semantics are guaranteed by the call site) and idempotent under retry.
evidence: ABSENCE: no mention of file-write atomicity (e.g., write-to-temp-then-rename), locking, or idempotency guarantee under concurrent writes
The existing FAIL sidecar path is implicitly assumed to have the same behavior, but the plan does not reference any existing atomicity mechanism being reused
findings.finding: The plan does not specify atomicity or concurrency safety for writing the PASS sidecar. If two verifier runs execute concurrently against the same ticket (e.g., parallel CI jobs or a retry race), the sidecar write is a check-then-act race with no locking or CAS semantics described.
scenarios: Two CI runners verify the same ticket simultaneously; one writes a partial sidecar that the other then overwrites mid-write, corrupting the record
A retry starts before the first run's sidecar write completes
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope description for `completion_sidecar.py`: 'Sidecar writes MUST be atomic (write to a `.tmp` file then `os.replace`) to be safe under concurrent or retried runs.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assert the post-rollback schema STATE explicitly: confirm that the `completion_verdict` schema uses `additionalProperties: true` (or equivalent lenient mode), so already-written sidecar records containing `criteria[]` remain valid after the field is removed by a revert, and document this guarantee in the Compatibility & rollback section.
evidence: Plan states: 'Reverting stops the PASS sidecar and drops the field; existing records stay readable.'
Plan does not specify whether post-rollback schema validation is lenient (additionalProperties allowed) or strict (unknown fields rejected).
Schema bump is mentioned ('bump schema') without specifying whether the old schema version's validator will tolerate the new `criteria[]` key in already-written files.
POST-ROLLBACK SCHEMA STATE criterion: the plan asserts only that 'rollback exits' effectively, not what the validator does with pre-rollback records that carry the now-unknown field.
findings.finding: The plan's rollback claim ('existing records stay readable') does not assert the schema STATE after rollback for already-emitted PASS sidecars that contain the new `criteria[]` field. If the reverted code applies strict schema validation against the old (field-absent) schema, those persisted sidecar records will fail validation and become effectively unreadable — a silent data-accessibility loss the plan does not acknowledge or mitigate.
scenarios: Operator rolls back after some PASS sidecars have been emitted with `criteria[]`. The reverted code loads the old schema and runs strict JSON Schema validation (e.g., `additionalProperties: false`). Every previously-written PASS sidecar now fails validation, breaking the `latest_pass_record` reader and any audit tooling that queries those files.
A CI job that validates sidecar history against the schema starts failing after rollback, surfacing noise and potentially blocking unrelated work.
findings.suggested_fix: In `completion_verdict.schema.json`, set `"additionalProperties": true` (or omit `additionalProperties`, which defaults to permissive in JSON Schema). Add a one-line note in the rollback section: 'Because the schema is permissive to unknown fields, previously-emitted PASS sidecars containing `criteria[]` remain schema-valid after revert; no reader breakage occurs.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the exhaustive allowed values for `criteria[].kind` (e.g. `"codebase-verifiable" | "operator-attested"`) as a schema enum in the `completion_verdict.schema.json` `criteria[]` item definition.
evidence: Plan Scope: 'add an OPTIONAL `criteria[]` array (`{criterion, met, citation, kind}`)'
ABSENCE: no enum or list of allowed values for `kind` is defined anywhere in the plan, in completion_verdict.schema.json (confirmed empty of this field), or in common.schema.json
The verifier prompt names 'codebase-verifiable' and 'operator-attested' as criterion kinds, but these appear in a classification context, not as the schema-bound enum for criteria[].kind
findings.finding: The `kind` field of the new `criteria[]` entry objects is referenced in the plan (`{criterion, met, citation, kind}`) but its allowed value vocabulary is never defined. The plan informally names two kinds (codebase-verifiable / operator-attested) in the verifier prompt, but neither the schema addition to completion_verdict.schema.json nor any part of the plan specifies these as the exhaustive enum, leaving an executor unable to determine valid values for `kind`.
findings.location: Scope — `src/rebar/schemas/completion_verdict.schema.json` / plan phrase `{criterion, met, citation, kind}`
scenarios: An executor implementing the schema adds `kind` as a free string, making the field unvalidatable and LLM output unpredictable
The LLM emits 'codebase' vs 'codebase-verifiable' and different callers interpret them differently
findings.suggested_fix: In the schema addition, use `"enum": ["codebase-verifiable", "operator-attested"]` for `criteria[].kind`, matching the two-way classification already defined in the verifier prompt.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
